name: Phishing Attack Response
description: Comprehensive playbook for responding to phishing attacks including spear phishing, whaling, and smishing. Covers identification, containment, eradication and recovery with detailed procedures for email analysis and credential protection.

phases.description: The purpose of the Identification stage is to accurately determine the incident type, severity and perform immediate containment and escalation.

tasks.title: Validate Phishing Incident
tasks.content:
Phishing is the fraudulent attempt to obtain sensitive information such as usernames, passwords and credit card details, by disguising oneself as a trustworthy entity in an email or phone text message.

Varying forms of phishing attacks exist:
- Spear phishing is targeted at an individual or specific group, typically with access to sensitive information or the ability to transfer funds. Information is gathered by cybercriminals about the intended target and leveraged to personalize the attack, creating a sense of familiarity and make the malicious email seem trustworthy.
- Whaling is a spear-phishing attack that specifically targets senior executives at an organization.
- Smishing uses text messages to try and persuade victims into revealing account information or installing malware.

Employees should be educated on reporting any suspected phishing attempts and not click on any links found in suspicious emails or phone texts. While many phishing campaigns are generic, spear phishing and whaling attempts are more critical to contain and eradicate as they are specifically targeting the organization.
tasks.description: Confirm security event is phishing and notify organization
outcomes: A security event is confirmed as a phishing attack
Organization is notified of targeted phishing campaign

subtasks.content:
Reviewed reported email as suspected phish. This required reviewing the emails for the following indicators:
• Strange names and email addresses
• Links to external and unknown URLs
• Words that depict urgency or importance such as 'Urgent', 'Mandatory', 'Critical', 'Restricted', 'Confirm Immediately' or 'Review Now'
• Spelling and grammatical mistakes
• Generic greetings
• Requests for personal or financial information
• Attachments including fake invoices, fake fax documents, fake voice messages
Once confirmed as phishing attempt, sent corporate wide message to warn employees of any general widely used phishing campaign or targeted campaigns against organization.
If the email is verified as legitimate, marked the event as a false positive and resolved the event.

tasks.title: Confirm Phishing Purpose
tasks.content:
Phishing attacks are typically utilized to steal credentials, install malware and/or spyware or initiate ransomware attacks. The emails may contain URLs leading to a malicious website replicating legitimate web resources, tricking victims into downloading malicious files or providing employee username/password credentials. It is critical to identify the source of the phishing attack and to determine what the end goal of the phishing campaign is so that effective containment and eradication steps can be performed.

The phishing emails headers contain valuable information that help determine the source of the attack. Other techniques used to determine phishing campaign goals include checking hashes against known phishing emails, checking malicious links against known databases and researching IP addresses and domain names linked with the phishing campaign. Gathering as much evidence as possible during the incident identification stage is crucial for future forensic activities, accurate record-keeping and ongoing triage activities.
tasks.description: Analyze email headers and determine attack purpose and source
outcomes: The purpose of the phishing email is determined
The source of the phishing attack is established

subtasks.content:
Analyzed email headers. If using Outlook, opened the email in a separate window and navigate to 'File' -> Properties' to view the properties. Relevant email headers will be displayed under 'Internet headers'. The following fields were analyzed:
· The 'received' headers to find the source of the phishing email
· X-apparently to: This field contains recipient address when BCC is used
· 'From' or 'x-sender' header which contains information about the email account used
· The mail server's IP address from where the phishing email was sent

Identified all URLs in the phishing email:
· Resolved the DNS name into an IP address using tools such as 'nslookup' or 'dig'
· Used IP reputation feeds to gain more information on the associated IP address(es)

Used a sandboxed testing machine that is not connected to the organization's network to retrieve the contents of the phishing URL. After retrieving the contents, identify the purpose of the URL (phishing employees, web application replication, malicious download, web exploit, etc.)
Note: This action requires subject matter expertise and should only have been performed if there is absolute certainty that it is being performed in an isolated environment.
Preserved all forensic evidence by using the Upload Artifact feature of this Task.

tasks.title: Initiate CIRT Team Meeting and Execute Escalation Procedures
tasks.content:
As more information about the incident is known, the core CIRT team will meet and define which organizational SMEs need to be included to respond to the incident. During the meeting, an initial assessment is made to determine what type of communication channel needs to be set up and what the periodic meeting cadence should be.

For any digital communications, it is best to assume complete compromise and utilize out-of-band communications mechanisms. Incident response escalation procedures are activated once some preliminary assessments are made. Typical escalation procedures would first include informing the corporate general council to create legal privilege.
tasks.description: Setup war room and establish secure communications
outcomes: First Core CIRT meeting is held with invited SMEs
OOB communication channels are set up and periodic checkins scheduled
Privileged Communications is established with legal counsel

subtasks.content: Initiated first meeting of the organizational core CIRT.
Notified CIRT team members using the contacts and role assignments and made sure ORNA roles are assigned.
Verified the Incident Severity determined during the Incident record creation process as promptly and accurately as possible. The severity may change as more details are uncovered.
Invited additional SMEs to be included in CIRT update meetings.
Set up a secured meeting room (either physical and/or virtual).
Scheduled follow-up meetings according to a defined cadence.
Worked closely with Legal Counsel to retain client-attorney privilege over the information pertaining to the incident and ensure compliance with applicable laws and regulations.
Created an initial incident assessment to share with organizational leaders containing summary of what occurred, date & time, scope, data involved, actions taken and next steps.
Established a log of items covered to keep track of meetings, attendees, discussion notes and follow-up actions.

tasks.title: Initiate Added Playbooks
tasks.content: If the phishing attack has been successful and subsequently created a malware or ransomware attack or employee username/password credentials have been compromised, the relevant incident response playbook needs to be initiated.
tasks.description: Trigger additional playbooks if phishing led to other attacks
outcomes: Additional incident response playbooks are initiated

subtasks.content: Initiated added incident response playbooks if the phishing campaign was discovered to successfully create a malware attack, ransomware attack or get access to employees' username/password credentials.

tasks.title: Determine Scope of Attack
tasks.content: Phishing attacks are common security events and many email tools exist to filter out phishing messages. Employees should be made aware that reporting all phishing messages is necessary so that tools can be updated. Employee reporting of phishing attacks can also help ascertain whether the attack is targeted specifically to the organization. The scope of general phishing attacks is hard to ascertain, however, targeted organizational attacks usually have the same message characteristics and with organizational wide employee notification and reviews of email servers the affected employees should be able to be identified.
tasks.description: Identify all affected employees and determine attack scope
outcomes: All affected employees of targeted phishing attack are identified

subtasks.content:
Reviewed all detection channels for indicators of recent phishing emails:
· Non-deliverable emails
· Reports from internal users about suspicious emails
· Reports from external users and clients
· Notifications from the email provider
· Notifications from 3rd parties, law enforcement or ISP
Reviewed replies from corporate wide message that was sent during 'Validate Phishing Incident' to warn employees.

Analyzed all corporate emails:
- Reviewed the email logs of the email gateway server and filtered for logs associated with the sender's email, malicious URLs or attachments
- Exported the email list and documented it as evidence
- Looked for links that do not point to the same URLs displayed (URL masking)
- Checked the email domain in the 'From:' field corresponds to the topic
- Looked for suspicious email subject lines
Updated antivirus (AV) signatures with the newly found hash/hashes and scanned the environment for these hashes to identify all other infected hosts.
Determined how many employees visited a malicious URL or downloaded the attachment by checking network logs for the specified URL or file name.
Preserved all forensic evidence by using the Upload Artifact feature.

tasks.title: Assess Impact
tasks.content:
Assess the impact to prioritize actions. The impacts should be assessed for:
- Any operational business impact which would affect the organization being able to function
- Any regulatory impact due to unavailability of systems or data
- Any reputational impact concerns with customer trust
tasks.description: Evaluate operational, financial and reputational impact

subtasks.content:
Assessed impact to business or mission:
• How critical is the data to the business/mission?
• How much money is lost or at risk?
• How many (and which) projects are degraded or at risk?
Assessed regulatory impact.

Assessed reputational damage:
• What type of data or service was inaccessible?
• Was the attack avoidable?

phases.description: The main objective of the Containment stage is to limit the impact of the incident promptly and as much as possible, preventing further damage to the organization's assets, business functions, individuals, and reputation.

tasks.title: Request a Malicious Domain Takedown
tasks.content: A malicious domain takedown is a process of disabling a domain name from being active. While an organization can go through the process itself, the difficulty of knowing exactly what evidence to present in varying global jurisdictions has created an industry around domain takedown services. These services exist to handle the domain takedown requests since there has to be sufficient proof that a domain is being used for criminal purposes. Requesting to take down the malicious domain or webpage will not only protect your employees, but other potential victims as well.
tasks.description: Request takedown of malicious domain or webpage
outcomes: Malicious domain takedown request is submitted
The malicious domain is taken down

subtasks.content: Decided whether to request a takedown from a hosting company or registrar or to use a domain takedown service.

Sent a domain takedown request. If handled internally, contacted the hosting company or registrar. If using external service, contacted the service. The evidence necessary for a domain takedown will vary across jurisdictions and was collected as part of this task.
Preserved all forensic evidence by using the Upload Artifact feature.

tasks.title: Block Malicious Artifacts
tasks.content: Malicious artifacts related to phishing emails can be in a form of a URL, attachment, sender's email, domain name, or IP address. Blocking malicious artifacts helps contain the spread of the phishing emails and minimizes the impact. The information collected during the Identification stage should be used for this task.
tasks.description: Blacklist malicious domains, IPs, and email addresses

subtasks.content: Blacklisted the sender domain, IP address and marked the email as spam (this will prevent additional users from being phished while the containment procedures are underway).
Created a DNS sinkhole for the malicious domain. A DNS sinkhole is a technique that can be used to disable a phishing website without taking any action against the hosting company or registrar.
Blocked the IP addresses and DNS names associated with the phishing emails using network firewall and DNS firewall rules.
Preserved all forensic evidence by using the Upload Artifact feature.

tasks.title: Lock Affected Accounts and Force Password Resets
tasks.content: In some cases, an attacker will use phishing emails to steal the victim's credentials, gain access to their account(s) and subsequently move laterally across the network. It is crucial to lock all affected accounts and force password resets to prevent the adversary from accessing any sensitive information. In cases where malware is delivered as an attachment, locking down affected users' accounts and resetting their passwords prevents the malware from using those accounts to spread across the network.
tasks.description: Secure compromised accounts through locking and password resets

subtasks.content: If a user clicked on a malicious URL and/or provided their credentials or other sensitive information, temporarily locked this user's account and performed a password reset.
Preserved all forensic evidence by using the Upload Artifact feature.

phases.description: The Eradication stage focuses on removing all traces of the threat from the affected assets, and ensuring that the root cause has been eliminated before the assets of affected business functions can be restored to normal.

tasks.title: Remove Phishing Emails
tasks.content: Once all phishing emails and their corresponding IoCs are identified, it is important to remove those emails from all users' mailboxes and all cloud or on-premise servers. Additionally, all downloaded attachments should be removed. These steps will prevent anyone from interacting with the malicious content.
tasks.description: Delete phishing emails from all mailboxes and servers
outcomes: All phishing emails are removed from all users' mailboxes
All phishing emails are deleted from cloud and on premise servers

subtasks.content: In Exchange/Office 365, added the admin user (that will be deleting the emails) to the Discovery Management role group. Administrators are not assigned this role by default.

Created and ran a Compliance Search to find the email:
- Opened the Exchange Management Shell
- Ran: New-ComplianceSearch -Name 'Remove Phishing Message' -ExchangeLocation all -ContentMatchQuery 'subject:"Subject of malicious email"'
- Ran: Start-ComplianceSearch -Identity 'Remove Phishing Message'

Deleted the relevant emails:
- Ran the PowerShell command: New-ComplianceSearchAction -SearchName 'Remove Phishing Message' -Purge -PurgeType SoftDelete
Configured the organizational email anti-spam software to block similar types of attachments, keywords, domains or senders. Popular anti-spam software options include SpamTitan and ZeroSpam.
Preserved all forensic evidence by using the Upload Artifact feature.

tasks.title: Update Email SPAM Filter Rules
tasks.content: As phishing campaigns continue to increase in sophistication, the SPAM filters will also need to be reviewed and updated on a periodic basis. This is especially critical after handling a successful phishing attack incident to ensure that a recurrence from a similar event will not occur.
tasks.description: Update anti-spam filters to prevent similar attacks

subtasks.content: Configured email anti-spam software to block similar types of attachments, keywords, domains or senders.

phases.description: The Recovery stage aims to restore the affected assets to their full business-as-usual operation in the shortest possible time frame, after eradicating all traces of the threat and verifying that the systems are clean.

tasks.title: Enable Additional Email Security Measures
tasks.content:
The use of Sender Policy Framework (SPF), DomainKeys Identified Mail (DKIM) and Domain-based Message Authentication, Reporting and Conformance (DMARC) can help protect against email phishing attacks and should be considered if they are not already implemented.

SPF is used to restrict who can send emails from your domain. SPF can prevent domain spoofing and it enables your mail server to determine when a message came from the domain that it uses.

DKIM ensures that the content of your emails remains trusted and hasn't been tampered with or compromised.

DMARC ties SPF and DKIM together with a consistent set of policies and links the sender's domain name with what is listed in the From: header.
tasks.description: Deploy SPF, DKIM and DMARC email authentication
outcomes: Ascertain whether SPF DKIM and DMAEC are deployed
Enabled SPF DKIM and DMARC is not yet deployed

subtasks.content: Reviewed whether SPF, DKIM and DMARC are deployed.

If not yet deployed, enabled SPF, DKIM and DMARC:
- Configured an SPF DNS Record (text record with IP addresses allowed to send emails)
- Configured a DKIM DNS record
- Configured the DMARC policy and reporting

tasks.title: Review Corporate Authentication Practices
tasks.content:
Many phishing attacks target gaining access to employee username/password credentials. Requiring the use of a password manager will help alleviate some issues where users reuse passwords on multiple sites. Good password managers include 1Password, Lastpass and Dashlane.

It is also important to enable multi-factor authentication where the compromise of a password will not result in successful unauthorized device access attempts. Businesses can choose from a variety of two-factor authentication providers, including OneLogin, Yubico and Okta.
tasks.description: Implement password managers and multi-factor authentication
outcomes: All employees are utilizing a password manager
Multi factor authentication is used for all device and service access

subtasks.content: Reviewed whether employees are required to use a password manager.
Created a plan to review and deploy a password manager if one is not already used by corporate employees.
Reviewed all service and device access to determine where multi-factor authentication is not utilized.
Created a plan to deploy multi-factor authentication where not yet deployed.

phases.description: The Lessons Learned stage's main goal is to compile all relevant information about the incident and extract lessons that can help with future incident response activity, typically within the two weeks time frame after the incident has been resolved.

tasks.title: Generate Incident Report
tasks.content: Preparing an incident report is an important (and often mandatory) part of the cyber incident response process. This report will be used to perform business impact analysis, comply with the applicable laws and regulations, update relevant stakeholders (e.g. legal counsel, insurance provider, law enforcement, board of directors, etc.) and for other business functions.
tasks.description: Create comprehensive incident report for stakeholders

subtasks.content: Once the Incident was marked as Resolved, used the Reports tab to generate and view the report.
Reviewed the report for accuracy and address any errors/issues with the team member(s) responsible for the task in question.
Ensured all relevant artifacts were included for each task.
Downloaded or exported the report and shared it with the appropriate stakeholders alongside its unique secure access password.
Ensured that the Incident report and all related evidence were retained in accordance with legal and regulatory requirements.

tasks.title: Fulfill External and Internal Reporting Obligations
tasks.content: Once the incident is resolved and the investigation is complete, an organization may be required to deliver the incident report (or its appropriate sections) to the regulatory agencies, affected companies or individuals, law enforcement or other stakeholders based on the applicable laws and regulations.
tasks.description: Submit required reports to regulatory authorities
outcomes: The Incident Report is shared with the applicable parties
Applicable regulatory and legal obligations are fulfilled

subtasks.content: Obtained the full Incident Report from the CIRT Leader and reviewed it for accuracy.
Identified all applicable legal and regulatory requirements related to cyber incident reporting.
Delivered the Incident report to the appropriate legal and/or regulatory agencies and the affected individuals, in accordance with the applicable legal and regulatory requirements.

tasks.title: Host the Lessons Learned Session
tasks.content: The goal of the Lessons Learned session is to learn from the incident, identify any policy, process or procedure gaps that were revealed during the handling of the incident, and to create initiatives to improve the organizations' security posture. The Lessons Learned session should be held as soon as possible, ideally within 2 weeks after the incident was resolved.
tasks.description: Conduct post-incident review with CIRT team
outcomes: The Lessons Learned session is conducted
Gaps and areas for improvement are identified
Initiatives are created to operationalize identified improvements

subtasks.content: Prepared a presentation for the Lessons Learned meeting, containing: when incident was detected, scope, response steps taken, areas of effectiveness, gaps and improvement opportunities, steps to prevent similar incidents.
Hosted the Lessons Learned session and ensured all CIRT members attend and actively participate.

Established an open dialogue discussing: How well staff and management handled the incident, whether procedures were followed and adequate, what needs to be done differently, whether 3rd parties were responsive, what modifications are necessary in policies and procedures, what training may be required.
Documented all identified areas for improvement and prepared a list of agreed upon action items.
Distributed the action items list among relevant CIRT members and ensured that the improvement steps are implemented.

tasks.title: Perform Employee Security Awareness Training
tasks.content: Whether the incident resulted from a phishing campaign, configuration error or from an exploited vulnerability, it is always a good idea to conduct an employee awareness training session after any incident is resolved. While the session should not be focused exclusively on the recent phishing incident, the mechanisms used to enable phishing attacks can be called out. The training can emphasize the risks and consequences of cyber incidents, encourage a security minded culture and reinforce how employees can report security related issues and concerns.
tasks.description: Conduct security awareness training for all employees
outcomes: The employee awareness training is conducted
A roadmap for table top exercises is developed based on lessons learnt

subtasks.content: Prepared educational materials for the employee awareness training, with emphasis on the recent incident and the steps employees can take to help the organization avoid similar incidents in the future.
Tracked employee attendance using a Learning Management System (LMS).
Where applicable, worked with a 3rd party to prepare and conduct the employee security awareness training.

Used the outcomes from the Lessons Learned part of the Incident Management Process to develop a table-top exercise plan with relevant scenarios and participants to practice any new processes developed post incident.

name: 3rd Party Breach Response
description: Comprehensive playbook for responding to third-party vendor or partner data breaches. Provides a clear, concise and systematic approach to responding to the breach, minimizing the impact, and restoring normal operations as quickly as possible.

phases.description: The purpose of the Identification stage is to accurately determine the incident type, severity and perform immediate containment and escalation.

tasks.title: Confirm the Breach
tasks.content: When a 3rd party experiences a cyber breach, it is crucial to accurately analyze and examine the details of the breach to understand the impact and the severity of the security risks to the organization resulting from this breach.
tasks.description: Verify 3rd party breach and determine organizational impact
outcomes: Scope of the breach is identified and understood

subtasks.content: Conducted interviews with relevant stakeholders from the affected 3rd party, either in person, by email, phone or a virtual meeting, to confirm the breach and collect relevant information.

Identified the affected systems, data and users managed by, or shared with the affected 3rd party, and reviewed relevant logs and alerts to identify whether any malicious activity associated with the 3rd party data breach has affected the organization's assets.
Recorded and uploaded all relevant information collected from the affected 3rd party in the "notes" section and/or by uploading artifacts.

tasks.title: Secure the Affected Systems
tasks.content: Once the 3rd party compromise is confirmed and the affected systems, data or users are identified, the organization must ensure that all affected assets are properly secured, to prevent the breach from moving laterally within the environment, and minimize the impact.
tasks.description: Isolate and secure systems affected by 3rd party breach
outcomes: Systems and data are secured from further damage
Evidence is preserved for further investigation

subtasks.content: Isolated the affected systems from the network. Provided the details about the specific isolation actions in the "notes" section.

phases.description: The main objective of the Containment stage is to limit the impact of the incident promptly and as much as possible, preventing further damage to the organization's assets, business functions, individuals, and reputation.

tasks.title: Restrict access of the affected 3rd party
tasks.content: All software, hardware, personnel and digital assets of the affected 3rd party should be restricted, if possible, or assigned the lowest privileges within the organization's corporate infrastructure, to avoid possible lateral movement or unauthorized access.
tasks.description: Limit 3rd party access to prevent further compromise

subtasks.content: Temporarily restricted the affected 3rd party digital and physical assets from accessing the network. Recorded the details about the restriction activities taken, in the task-level notes.

phases.description: The Eradication stage focuses on removing all traces of the threat from the affected assets, and ensuring that the root cause has been eliminated before the assets of affected business functions can be restored to normal.

tasks.title: Confirm that the 3rd party addressed the breach
tasks.content: When 3rd parties experience cyber breaches, it is important to stay in close contact and continue openly communicating about the remediation steps they are taking to eradicate the root cause of the breach, and taking additional measures to compensate for the damages or additional risks that were introduced as a result of such breach.
tasks.description: Verify 3rd party remediation and compensatory controls
outcomes: Confirmation of breach remediation is received

subtasks.content: Received a satisfactory confirmation from the 3rd party about the remediation and compensation steps the 3rd party is taking to protect the organization's assets. Added the details of such measures/responses in the Artifacts or task-level notes.

phases.description: The Recovery stage aims to restore the affected assets to their full business-as-usual operation in the shortest possible time frame, after eradicating all traces of the threat and verifying that the systems are clean.

tasks.title: Restore 3rd party access as needed
tasks.content: After it was confirmed that the cyber breach is remediated by the affected 3rd party, and the satisfactory compensation controls have been implemented, it is important to restore the required access of the 3rd party assets to the organization's infrastructure to return to the BAU (Business As Usual) state and resume normal operations.
tasks.description: Restore third-party access and reconnect systems

subtasks.content: Restored access that was restricted during the Containment stage. Included the details about the access in the task-level notes.
Restored connectivity and functionality of the affected systems, data and users that were contained during the investigation and containment stages of this incident.

tasks.title: Communicate with Affected Parties
tasks.content:
An organization should issue notifications to customers if its partner or vendor suffered a cyber breach, even if the organization itself and its customers are not affected, for several reasons:

1. Transparency: Customers trust organizations to protect their personal data and privacy. Issuing a notification demonstrates transparency and a commitment to keeping customers informed about potential risks, even if they are not directly impacted.

2. Responsibility: Organizations have a responsibility to ensure the security of their supply chain, which includes their vendors and partners. Issuing a notification demonstrates that the organization takes this responsibility seriously and is actively monitoring their supply chain for potential risks.

3. Prevention: Customers may have shared personal data with the vendor or partner, which could potentially be used in future attacks. Issuing a notification can help customers take preventative measures, such as changing passwords or monitoring their financial accounts, to reduce the risk of future attacks.

4. Legal Requirements: Some countries and industries have legal requirements for breach notifications, which may apply even if the organization itself and its customers are not directly impacted. Failure to comply with these requirements can result in legal and reputational consequences.
tasks.description: Notify affected parties about breach and remediation

subtasks.content: Notified affected parties, including customers, employees, and third-party vendors, and provided the details of the breach and actions taken to remediate the breach.

phases.description: The Lessons Learned stage's main goal is to compile all relevant information about the incident and extract lessons that can help with future incident response activity, typically within the two weeks time frame after the incident has been resolved.

tasks.title: Conduct the Lessons Learn session with the affected 3rd party
tasks.content: After the cyber breach has been remediated, and the cooperation with the 3rd party has been restored to the state of BAU, it is important to meet with the relevant stakeholders of the 3rd party and agree on the steps the 3rd party will be taking to improve their state of cybersecurity, prevent future cyber breaches, and how they will protect the organization's data going forward. It may be useful to establish a regular cadence with the 3rd party to monitor their progress in implementing the promised cybersecurity improvements.
tasks.description: Review incident with 3rd party and establish improvement plan
outcomes: The Lessons Learned session is conducted
Action items and next steps are identified

subtasks.content:
Met with the 3rd party stakeholders and received appropriate reassurances about the cybersecurity improvements, timelines and guarantees aimed at preventing future cyber breaches and protecting the organization's data.
Included details of the meeting and the information either in Artifacts and/or in task-level notes.

name: Other/Hybrid Incident Response
description: Comprehensive playbook for responding to hybrid incidents or attacks that are difficult to identify. Provides systematic approach to handling unknown or complex cyber incidents.

phases.description: The purpose of the Identification stage is to accurately determine the incident type, severity and perform immediate containment and escalation.

tasks.title: Setup the War Room
tasks.content: When an incident occurs, it is critically important to set up a secure communication channel (sometimes called the War Room) to allow secure cross-team communications. War Rooms are designed to bring key decision-makers together and arm them with all the information necessary to make rapid decisions during high-risk situations. The War Room will be used for sharing all incident-related information between the CIRT members, speeding up the decision-making process and simplifying record-keeping.
tasks.description: Establish secure physical and virtual war room for incident response
outcomes: War Room and out of band communication channels are set up
Access to relevant CIRT members is established

subtasks.content:
Set up a physical War Room, if applicable: typically, a boardroom inside your office building, access to which is provided to the CIRT members only (for example, accessible only with the use of select few access cards) can be repurposed as a war room for the duration of the incident response activities.

Secured the War Room to ensure that no information shared within can be accessed from the outside (e.g. if the boardroom has glass walls, those may need to be covered to restrict the visibility from the hallway).

Set up a virtual War Room, if applicable: selected a communications platform that is not typically used by the organization (e.g. if MS Teams is used for regular business communications, it should not be used as a virtual War Room in case internal communications channels are compromised). Secure the virtual War Room by restricting access to any non-CIRT members.

tasks.title: Inform CIRT Members and Perform Triage
tasks.content: When an incident occurs, it is critically important to accurately triage it: in other words, collect as much relevant and reliable information as possible. This includes verifying the Incident Severity, its Type, the vulnerability or weakness that was exploited, and, if possible, the exact malware or a threat actor behind the attack. Equally important is the escalation and notification speed. All relevant CIRT members must be notified and briefed as soon as possible in order to start investigation and short-term containment procedures. Consider the need to contact third parties as well, such as your IT Services provider, your insurer, or external forensic investigators where required.
tasks.description: Notify CIRT team and establish incident type and severity
outcomes: CIRT Members are notified and engaged in Response activities
Incident Severity and Type are established and confirmed

subtasks.content: Verified the Incident Severity determined during the Incident record creation process as promptly and accurately as possible. Once the Severity was confirmed, made sure to keep updating it accordingly when the situation changes.

Notified CIRT team members using the contacts and role assignments. Invited all relevant CIRT team members into the war room, whether physical or virtual. Make sure their roles are assigned so they are aware of the tasks allocated for them.
Considered retaining a PCI Forensic Investigator (PFI) if a cardholder data breach is suspected.
Tried to determine the root cause of the Incident (e.g. an unpatched vulnerability, excessive account privileges) and mitigate it, if possible.
Try to establish the malware family or a particular threat actor responsible for the attack using available Indicators of Compromise.
Immediately notified your Insurer (or Insurance Broker) and worked on the next steps closely to ensure coverage.

tasks.title: Notify Legal Counsel, Insurance Provider and Law Enforcement
tasks.content: Once the incident type and severity are established and confirmed, it is important to involve appropriate parties, such as your legal counsel, insurance providers and/or law enforcement, depending on the level of impact.
tasks.description: Notify stakeholders and ensure legal compliance
outcomes: Legal Counsel and cybersecurity insurance provider/broker are notified

subtasks.content: Notified your Legal Counsel about the ongoing incident, provided all available details about the incident severity, impact and the type of information involved, if known.
Worked closely with your Legal Counsel to retain client-attorney privilege over the information pertaining to the incident and ensured compliance with applicable laws and regulations.
Immediately notified your Insurer (or Insurance Broker) and work on the next steps closely to ensure coverage.
Depending on the impact and the details of the incident (e.g. evidence of insider threat), and, following the advised of the Legal Counsel, considered contacting appropriate law enforcement agencies.

tasks.title: Issue Internal and External Communications
tasks.content: It is critical to establish proactive communication with your staff and provide clear instructions about what they can do to minimize the impact. Ensure that employees receive the information about the incident directly from your communication leader and/or management team, preferably before they receive it from other, unreliable sources. Provide clear instructions about what they are allowed and not allowed to communicate externally. Controlling the communications will prevent unwanted media attention and misinformation.
tasks.description: Manage internal and external crisis communications
outcomes: Employees are notified about the incident and instructed on the next steps
External communications to customers, other stakeholders and/or media are made

subtasks.content: Prepared internal and external communications and consulted the Legal Counsel prior to sharing with any additional parties.

Published external communications, if appropriate, in line with the communications strategy to provided advice to affected customers or business partners, engaged with the market, and/or informed the public of the cyber incident.
Considered regulatory requirements when issuing incident updates including PIPEDA, GDPR, PCI DSS, NYDFS, FFIEC, NCUA, OSFI, GLBA, SOX, HIPAA/PHIPA and other applicable regulatory frameworks.
Deployed the alert/warning page containing information about the current incident to inform employees. Used crisis-only communications channels if they exist.

tasks.title: Invoke Data Breach Notification Protocols
tasks.content: When a cyber incident is suspected or confirmed, it is important that the Legal Counsel proceeds swiftly from the very first moments of the initial identification to the initial stages of the post-breach investigation. Understanding the type of the incident and the kind of data affected by a possible breach will dictate which notification protocols need to be invoked to ensure continuous compliance with applicable regulations, laws and contractual obligations.
tasks.description: Determine and execute data breach notification requirements
outcomes: The type of breach and affected data are established and confirmed
Appropriate data breach notification protocols are invoked

subtasks.content: Worked with the Communications Leader on the verbiage of applicable internal and external communications.

If a data breach is confirmed in regards to Personal Identifiable Information (PII), Protected Health Information (PHI), or financial information, notified the appropriate regulatory agencies and affected individuals as prescribed by relevant laws, regulations and contractual obligations.
Advised the CIRT members on proper communication methods within and outside the War Room to ensure continuous client-attorney privilege where possible.

phases.description: The main objective of the Containment stage is to limit the impact of the incident promptly and as much as possible, preventing further damage to the organization's assets, business functions, individuals, and reputation.

tasks.title: Invoke the Business Continuity Plan
tasks.content: To avoid major disruptions of key business functions, invoke the Business Continuity Plan (BCP), if such exists, in accordance with the incident severity and impact. The BCP will aid the executive team in performing business impact analysis and driving the incident activities based on the Maximum Tolerable Downtime (MTD), Recovery Point Objective (RPO), Recovery Time Objective (RTO), and Work Recovery Time (WRT).
tasks.description: Activate BCP to minimize business disruption
outcomes: The Business Continuity Plan is invoked

subtasks.content: Issued the communication and informed the appropriate stakeholders that the BCP should be invoked to minimize business disruption.
Proceeded in accordance with the BCP procedures.

tasks.title: Issue External and Internal Communications Update
tasks.content: Issuing regular updates about the progress of the incident triage activities and the measures the organization is taking to contain and remediate the incident will help control the narrative and maintain trust with both internal and external stakeholders.
tasks.description: Provide status updates to stakeholders
outcomes: Internal stakeholders are informed about the ongoing containment measures
External stakeholders are informed about the ongoing containment measures

subtasks.content: Issued the communications update to internal stakeholders in accordance with the communications strategy.
Issued the communications update to external stakeholders and the media in accordance with the communications strategy.

tasks.title: Issue Mandatory Notifications or Updates
tasks.content: Issuing regular updates about the progress of the incident triage activities and the measures the organization is taking to contain and remediate the incident may be required based on the incident severity and the applicable laws, regulations and contractual obligations.
tasks.description: Fulfill regulatory notification requirements
outcomes: Applicable internal and external parties are informed about the ongoing containment measures

subtasks.content: Notified the appropriate regulatory agencies and affected individuals in accordance with the applicable laws and regulations as was done during the Identification stage.
Continued advising the CIRT members on proper communications methods within and outside the War Room to ensure maintaining continuous client-attorney privilege where possible.

phases.description: The Eradication stage focuses on removing all traces of the threat from the affected assets, and ensuring that the root cause has been eliminated before the assets of affected business functions can be restored to normal.

tasks.title: Identify and Mitigate Vulnerabilities
tasks.content: Once the affected systems have been fully rebuilt, it is important to identify any vulnerabilities that may be present on the systems, preventing similar attacks from happening in the future.
tasks.description: Scan for and remediate security vulnerabilities

subtasks.content:
Ran a vulnerability scan on the entire network and the surrounding infrastructure using specialized vulnerability scanning tools, such as:
- Nessus free trial (7 days, up to 32 IPs)
- Qualys free trial (30 days, unlimited IPs)
- OpenVAS open source scanner
Implemented all relevant mitigation recommendations provided by the vulnerability scanning tools.
Preserved forensic evidence of any discovered vulnerabilities using the Artifact Upload feature.
Preserved all forensic evidence by using the Upload Artifact feature.

phases.description: The Recovery stage aims to restore the affected assets to their full business-as-usual operation in the shortest possible time frame, after eradicating all traces of the threat and verifying that the systems are clean.

The Lessons Learned stage's main goal is to compile all relevant information about the incident and extract lessons that can help with future incident response activity, typically within the two weeks time frame after the incident has been resolved.

tasks.title: Generate Incident Report
tasks.content: Preparing an incident report is an important (and often mandatory) part of the cyber incident response process. This report will be used to perform business impact analysis, comply with the applicable laws and regulations, update relevant stakeholders (e.g. legal counsel, insurance provider, law enforcement, board of directors, etc.) and for other business functions.
tasks.description: Create comprehensive incident report

subtasks.content: Once the Incident is marked as Resolved, used the Reports tab to generate and view the report.
Reviewed the report for accuracy and address any errors/issues with the team member(s) responsible for the task in question.
Ensured all relevant artifacts are included for each task.
Downloaded or exported the report and shared it with the appropriate stakeholders alongside its unique secure access password.
Ensured that the Incident report and all related evidence are retained in accordance with legal and regulatory requirements.

tasks.title: Fulfill External and Internal Reporting Obligations
tasks.content: Once the incident is resolved and the investigation is complete, an organization may be required to deliver the incident report (or its appropriate sections) to the regulatory agencies, affected companies or individuals, law enforcement or other stakeholders based on the applicable laws and regulations.
tasks.description: Submit reports to regulatory and legal authorities
outcomes: The Incident Report (or its appropriate sections) is shared with the applicable parties
Applicable regulatory and legal obligations are fulfilled

subtasks.content: Obtained the full Incident Report from the CIRT Leader and reviewed it for accuracy.
Identified all applicable legal and regulatory requirements related to cyber incident reporting.
Delivered the Incident report to the appropriate legal and/or regulatory agencies and the affected individuals, in accordance with the applicable legal and regulatory requirements.

tasks.title: Host the Lessons Learned Session
tasks.content: The goal of the Lessons Learned session is to learn from the incident, identify any gaps that were revealed in the process of incident response, and to improve the team's performance in future incidents. The Lessons Learned session should be held as soon as possible, ideally within 2 weeks after the incident was resolved.
tasks.description: Conduct post-incident review and identify improvements
outcomes: The Lessons Learned session is held
Improvements to the incident response procedures are identified and documented

subtasks.content: Scheduled a Lessons Learned session within 2 weeks after the incident resolution.
Invited all relevant CIRT members to the session and requested that they come prepared with findings and recommendations.
Reviewed the findings from the incident and discussed the effectiveness of response procedures, identifying gaps and areas for improvement.
Documented all identified improvements and assigned responsible parties to implement them.

name: Suspected Virus Response
description: Comprehensive playbook for responding when a virus is suspected on a local or remote system. Covers identification through virus scans, isolation, malware removal, system recovery and lessons learned.

phases.description: The purpose of the Identification stage is to accurately determine the incident type, severity and perform immediate containment and escalation.

tasks.title: A Virus is Reported
tasks.content:
A virus may be reported through EDR alerts or user reports of suspicious computer behavior. Signs of a virus include:
• Suddenly slow computer performance
• Problems unexpectedly shutting down or restarting
• Missing files
• Frequent system crashes and error messages
• Unexpected pop-up windows
• New applications appearing without downloading them
• Overworked hard drive (fan whirring constantly)
• Emails sending automatically from accounts
• Lagging web browser or constant redirects
• Malfunctioning antivirus programs or firewalls
tasks.description: Detect virus through EDR or user reports and run virus scans
outcomes: A virus scan cannot be run

subtasks.content:
Does the user have TPF's AV installed?
If yes, check the Bitdefender security webconsole by searching for the Vault entry: 'Bit Defender'
In the Bit Defender web GUI, locate the asset. Were remediation actions taken?
Run full system scan with Bit Defender.
Run Ram scan.
If the system is so compromised that a virus scan cannot be completed, proceed to recovery.

phases.description: The main objective of the Containment stage is to limit the impact of the incident promptly and as much as possible, preventing further damage to the organization's assets, business functions, individuals, and reputation.

tasks.title: Disconnect Affected Assets from the Network
tasks.content:
Viruses can spread through the network without any user interaction. It is crucial to immediately disconnect all devices that have been infected from the rest of the corporate network, containing the infection and preventing the virus from spreading to additional devices.

Do NOT power down the affected devices, as this may lead to the loss of important forensic evidence. Also, if encrypted systems are powered down they may not restart.
tasks.description: Isolate infected systems to prevent virus spread

subtasks.content: Preserved forensic evidence, such as system images, before restarting, shutting down, or modifying configurations of the affected systems and assets.
Disconnected the infected systems, shared drives and devices from any network connection to reduce the risk of the infection spreading to other connected devices.

Blocked added communications associated with infected systems to contain the virus infection. Considerations include disabling virtual private network access, remote access servers, single sign on resources, and cloud-based or public-facing assets.
Attached the applicable forensics evidence (such as log files or screenshots) to this task using the Upload Artifact feature.

tasks.title: Scan the Network for Additional Malware
tasks.content: Many malware campaigns utilize various malware families that threat actors use to ensure persistence on the network or to perform additional tasks and monitor the organizations' actions during the incident response process. It is imperative to detect any additional malware and, if possible, eliminate it.
tasks.description: Search for and identify additional malware infections
outcomes: All additional malware is detected and eliminated

subtasks.content: Ensured that antivirus signatures were up to date and that the antivirus software is installed and enabled on all assets.
Confirmed that the latest patches, in particular those relating to security updates, were deployed on all assets (prioritizing the targeted devices, software, operating systems, etc.)

tasks.title: Protect Backups
tasks.content: The most critical aspect of virus recovery is having trusted backups, which will need immediate attention to ensure that they are protected and not overwritten.
tasks.description: Secure and verify backup integrity

subtasks.content: Analyze backup processes for specific systems and data that could have been impacted.
Check status of backup snapshots.

phases.description: The Eradication stage focuses on removing all traces of the threat from the affected assets, and ensuring that the root cause has been eliminated before the assets of affected business functions can be restored to normal.

tasks.title: Deep Scan to Remove Malware
tasks.content:
IT will attempt to remove viruses either by the deletion of folders on the drive or through a virus program such as Bit Defender or Malwarebytes.

Bit Defender should already be loaded on the system. A ram scan is initiated.

Malwarebytes can be downloaded from https://www.malwarebytes.com/mwb-download
tasks.description: Run deep scans with multiple tools to eradicate virus
outcomes: Malware is no longer present on the system

subtasks.content: Bit Defender deep scan is run on the system. Done from the Bit Defender web GUI, accessed inside Zoho Vault.

Malwarebytes deep scan is run on the system. This is for additional assurance the virus has been totally removed.
https://www.malwarebytes.com/mwb-download

A ram scan is run on the system.
Data can persist inside ram even if it removed from the hard disk. A full reset and ram scan should be initiated BEFORE booting into windows. This can be done from the Bit Defender web GUI, which is accessed via Vault.

phases.description: The Recovery stage aims to restore the affected assets to their full business-as-usual operation in the shortest possible time frame, after eradicating all traces of the threat and verifying that the systems are clean.

tasks.title: Restore From Backup
tasks.content:
Backup can be initiated either partially (from files) or a bare metal backup can be done (Restoring full system image and Windows install). IO safe contains backups from the last 10 days.

The type of backup done is dependent on the type of compromise.
tasks.description: Restore system from clean backup
outcomes: System is restored from bare metal backup

subtasks.content:
IT will need to restore the backup from IO safe 220+.
The IO access credentials and authenticator can be found within Zoho Vault.
In the IO Safe, IT will need to select the 'Active Backup for Business' application.
From there, individual files can be restored or OS recovery media can be created to restore the entire system to known good point.

tasks.title: Resolve Incident
tasks.content: Once the affected systems and data are restored to normal business operations the incident can be moved to the 'resolved' state.
tasks.description: Verify restoration and close incident
outcomes: The incident is declared as resolved

subtasks.content: Verified with Core CIRT Members and IT staff that all systems and configurations are back to normal business functions.
Changed status of incident to 'resolved'.

phases.description: The Lessons Learned stage's main goal is to compile all relevant information about the incident and extract lessons that can help with future incident response activity, typically within the two weeks time frame after the incident has been resolved.

tasks.title: Generate Incident Report
tasks.content: Preparing an incident report is an important (and often mandatory) part of the cyber incident response process. This report will be used to perform business impact analysis, comply with the applicable laws and regulations, update relevant stakeholders (e.g. legal counsel, insurance provider, law enforcement, board of directors, etc.) and for other business functions.
tasks.description: Create comprehensive incident report with forensic details

subtasks.content:
1. Executive Summary
One–two paragraphs describing what happened, when it was discovered, and the overall impact. A high-level overview of root cause and key corrective actions.

2. Incident Description
• Date/time of detection
• Who identified the issue
• System(s) affected (hostname, user, OS, network segment)
• Type of infection (malware, ransomware, trojan, PUP, etc.)
• Initial symptoms (slow performance, pop-ups, AV alerts, strange processes, file modifications)

3. Timeline of Events
Create a chronological list:
• When the system was infected (if known)
• When symptoms first appeared
• When IT/security was notified
• Actions taken during triage
• Escalation steps
• Containment, eradication, and recovery timestamps

4. Root Cause Analysis
Clearly identify:
• Entry vector (phishing email, malicious attachment, drive-by download, USB, RDP brute force, outdated plugin, etc.)
• Vulnerabilities exploited (unpatched OS, outdated antivirus, weak password, disabled protections)
• Any user actions that contributed (clicked link, installed unauthorized software)
Include evidence if available: Logs, AV scan results, EDR telemetry, Browser history, Email metadata

5. Impact Assessment
Document what the malware actually did or attempted to do:
• Data exposure (if any)
• Files encrypted or modified
• Lateral movement attempts
• Credential compromise risk
• Downtime experienced
• Business impact (lost productivity, delayed tasks, incident response time)
If nothing significant happened (common with commodity malware), state that explicitly.

tasks.title: Host the Lessons Learned Session
tasks.content:
The goal of the Lessons Learned session is to learn from the incident, identify any policy, process or procedure gaps that were revealed during the handling of the incident, and to create initiatives to improve the organizations' security posture.

The Lessons Learned session should be held as soon as possible, ideally within 2 weeks after the incident was resolved.

Think in terms of 'What allowed this infection to succeed?'
• Missing patches?
• Weak security configuration?
• Insufficient user training?
• Lack of logging or alerting?
• AV signatures outdated?
• No EDR installed?
tasks.description: Conduct post-incident review and identify improvements
outcomes: The Lessons Learned session is conducted
Gaps and areas for improvement are identified
Initiatives are created to operationalize identified improvements

subtasks.content:
Prepared a presentation for the Lessons Learned meeting, containing the following information:
• When the incident was first detected and by whom (or what)
• The scope of the incident
• Identification, Containment, Eradication and Recovery steps that were taken
• Areas where CIRT members were effective
• Gaps in the incident response process along with improvement opportunities
• Steps taken at this time to prevent similar incidents.
Hosted the Lessons Learned session. Make sure that all CIRT members attend and actively participate in the session.
Documented all identified areas for improvement and prepared a list of agreed upon action items.
Distributed the action items list among relevant CIRT members and ensured that the improvement steps are implemented.

tasks.title: Perform Employee Security Awareness Training
tasks.content:
Whether the incident resulted from a phishing campaign, configuration error or from an exploited vulnerability, it is always a good idea to conduct an employee awareness training session after any incident is resolved.

While the session should not be focused exclusively on the recent virus incident, the risks and methods of malware infection can be called out. The training can emphasize the risks and consequences of cyber incidents, encourage a security minded culture and reinforce how employees can report security related issues and concerns.
tasks.description: Conduct security awareness training for employees
outcomes: The employee awareness training is conducted
A roadmap for table top exercises is developed based on lessons learnt

subtasks.content: Prepared educational materials for the employee awareness training, with emphasis on the recent incident and the steps employees can take to help the organization avoid similar incidents in the future.
Tracked employee attendance using a Learning Management System (LMS).
Where applicable, worked with a 3rd party to prepare and conduct the employee security awareness training.

Used the outcomes from the Lessons Learned part of the Incident Management Process to develop a table top exercise plan with relevant scenarios and participants to practice any new processes developed post incident.

name: Ransomware Response
description: A malware that employs encryption to hold a victim's information at ransom. Critical data is encrypted so that files, databases, or applications cannot be accessed. A ransom is then demanded to provide access.

phases.name: Identification
phases.description: The purpose of the Identification stage is to accurately determine the Incident type, severity and perform immediate containment and escalation.

tasks.title: Determine the Type of Ransomware
tasks.content: When a ransomware incident occurs, it is critical to correctly identify the ransomware variant and, if possible, the threat actor behind the attack. This will allow for more effective containment and recovery procedures.
outcomes: Containment and Eradication methods are understood

subtasks.title: Review ransomware note(s)
subtasks.content: Reviewed ransomware note(s) which can include: text or html files, image files as wallpaper, contact emails in encrypted file extensions, pop-ups after trying to open encrypted files, voice or email messages.

subtasks.title: Analyze affected files
subtasks.content: Analyzed affected and/or new files to assess the following behaviors: file renaming scheme, file corruption vs encryption, targeted file types and locations, owning user/group of affected files, icon for encrypted files, file markers, existence of file listings, key files or other data files.

subtasks.title: Analyze affected software or system types
subtasks.content: Analyzed affected software or system types. Some ransomware variants only affect certain tools (e.g., databases) or platforms (e.g., NAS products).

subtasks.title: Review bitcoin address
subtasks.content: Reviewed bitcoin address.

subtasks.title: Utilize CryptoSheriff tool
subtasks.content: Utilized the CryptoSheriff tool from NoMoreRansom. [https://www.nomoreransom.org/crypto-sheriff.php?lang=en]

tasks.title: Initiate CIRT Team Meeting and Execute Escalation Procedures
tasks.content: As more information about the incident is known, the core CIRT team will meet and define which organizational SMEs need to be included to respond to the incident.
outcomes: First Core CIRT meeting is held with invited SMEs
OOB communications channels are set up and periodic checkins scheduled
Privileged Communications is established with legal counsel

subtasks.title: Initiate first CIRT meeting
subtasks.content: Initiated first meeting of the organizational core CIRT.

subtasks.title: Notify CIRT team members
subtasks.content: Notified CIRT team members using the contacts and role assignments on the My Team page and made sure ORNA roles are assigned so they are aware of the tasks allocated for them.

subtasks.title: Verify Incident Severity
subtasks.content: Verified the Incident Severity determined during the Incident record creation process as promptly and accurately as possible. The severity may change as more details are uncovered and it will need to be updated when the situation changes.

subtasks.title: Invite additional SMEs
subtasks.content: Invited additional SMEs to be included in CIRT update meetings.

subtasks.title: Set up secured meeting room
subtasks.content: Set up a secured meeting room (either physical and/or virtual).

subtasks.title: Schedule follow-up meetings
subtasks.content: Scheduled follow-up meetings according to a defined cadence.

subtasks.title: Work with Legal Counsel
subtasks.content: Worked closely with Legal Counsel to retain client-attorney privilege over the information pertaining to the incident and ensure compliance with applicable laws and regulations.

subtasks.title: Create initial incident assessment
subtasks.content: Created an initial incident assessment to share with organizational leaders. This assessment notification will contain a summary of what has occurred; date & time of incident; scope of compromise; data involved; actions taken so far and next steps recipients can expect to see.

subtasks.title: Establish log of meetings
subtasks.content: Established a log of items covered to keep track of when meetings occurred, who was present, high level discussion notes and follow-up actions.

tasks.title: Validate Ransomware Incident
tasks.content: Ransomware is usually a specific type of malware designed to encrypt files on systems to render them unavailable and unusable until a ransom is paid.
outcomes: A security incident is confirmed as a ransomware attack

subtasks.title: Validate ransomware message
subtasks.content: Validated any received ransomware message(s).

subtasks.title: Determine if files encrypted
subtasks.content: Determined whether system files have been encrypted.

subtasks.title: Identify new files
subtasks.content: Identified existence of any new files on impacted system(s). Ransomware can rename your data which is a way to create some confusion on which files are affected.

subtasks.title: Identify file extension alterations
subtasks.content: Identified any file extension alterations. Some ransomware will alter the filename extensions. If some filenames have new extensions, this can also help identify which ransomware family is being used in the attack.

subtasks.title: Determine file exfiltration
subtasks.content: Determined whether there has been successful file exfiltration (using mechanisms such as sFTP, WebDAV, DNS tunneling, etc).

tasks.title: Assess if Threat Actor is Live
tasks.content: When there is an active threat actor in the network the containment tasks should start in parallel with the rest of the identification phase to try and mitigate further damage as fast as possible.
outcomes: Determination when to start with the containment phase

subtasks.title: Review system logs
subtasks.content: Reviewed system logs for active unauthorized access including: open files lists on associated servers, file properties of encrypted files, event logs for RDP connections, Windows Security log, SMB event logs, and any related logs.

subtasks.title: Determine if files being encrypted
subtasks.content: Determined if any files are in midst of getting encrypted. Ran a network analyzer (e.g. Wireshark) on the impacted server with a filter to identify IP addresses involved in actively writing or renaming files.

subtasks.title: Determine if data being exfiltrated
subtasks.content: Determined if any data is being exfiltrated (using mechanisms such as sFTP, WebDAV, DNS tunneling, etc)

tasks.title: Execute Initial Containment Steps
tasks.content: Some initial containment steps need to be executed to ensure that there is no further damage to the organization from the ransomware attack. The most critical aspect is having trusted backups.

subtasks.title: Analyze backup processes
subtasks.content: Analyzed backup processes for specific systems and data that has been impacted.

subtasks.title: Review BC/DR processes
subtasks.content: Reviewed existing BC/DR processes within your organization if they exist.

subtasks.title: Disconnect infected systems
subtasks.content: Disconnect known infected systems from the network.

tasks.title: Determine Scope of Attack
tasks.content: All of the affected systems and applications need to be identified. It is important to collect as much relevant information as possible about all assets that are affected and how.
outcomes: All affected applications and files are identified

subtasks.title: Identify affected systems
subtasks.content: Identified which systems were affected by scanning for concrete indicators of compromise (IOCs), reviewing endpoint protection/EDR system logs, checking similar systems for infection, and looking for external command and control (C2).

subtasks.title: Determine affected data
subtasks.content: Determined what data is affected (e.g., file types, department or group, affected software). Found anomalous changes to file metadata such as mass changes to creation or modification times.

tasks.title: Assess Need for External Support
tasks.content: After an initial assessment of the scope of the ransomware attack the organization will have to make some determination as to whether outside help is needed and for what functions.
outcomes: Decision is made whether to hire external technical help
Decision is made whether to hire external legal help
ORNA incident response expertise is utilized if needed

subtasks.title: Consider PCI Forensic Investigator
subtasks.content: Considered retaining a PCI Forensic Investigator (PFI) if a cardholder data breach is suspected.

subtasks.title: Assess other forensics needs
subtasks.content: Assessed whether any other data compromise requires a certified forensics expert to be utilized.

subtasks.title: Consider forensics investigation company
subtasks.content: Considered retaining a forensics investigation company to help assess complete scope of the attack.

subtasks.title: Consider outside legal counsel
subtasks.content: Considered retaining an outside legal counsel.

subtasks.title: Consider ORNA support
subtasks.content: Considered retaining ORNA for incident response expertise.

tasks.title: Assess Impact
tasks.content: Assess the impact to prioritize actions. The impacts should be assessed for operational business impact, regulatory impact, and reputational impact.

subtasks.title: Assess business impact
subtasks.content: Assessed impact to business or mission: How critical is the data? How much money is lost or at risk? How many projects are degraded or at risk?

subtasks.title: Assess regulatory impact
subtasks.content: Assessed regulatory impact: How sensitive is the data? What is the regulatory status of data (e.g., PII, ePHI)?

subtasks.title: Assess reputational damage
subtasks.content: Assessed reputational damage: What type of data was compromised? Was the attack avoidable?

tasks.title: Decide Whether to Pay Ransom
tasks.content: Whether to pay a ransom fee is an organizational decision although there are jurisdictions that are discussing regulating the legality of paying a ransom. It is strongly encouraged that organizations notify Law Enforcement.
outcomes: Decision is made whether to pay ransom

subtasks.title: Review decryption key availability
subtasks.content: Reviewed whether decryption key available for type of ransomware utilized if file were encrypted.

subtasks.title: Assess with legal counsel
subtasks.content: Assessed with legal counsel whether to pay ransom.

subtasks.title: Decide on law enforcement contact
subtasks.content: Decided whether to contact law enforcement.

tasks.title: Determine Root Cause(s)
tasks.content: There exist a variety of methods that ransomware gangs use to create a successful attack. It is critical to understand the full extent of the ransomware campaign.

subtasks.title: Check MITRE ATT&CK tactics
subtasks.content: Checked the tactics captured in the Initial Access tactic of MITRE ATT&CK including: email attachments, insecure RDP, self-propagation, infection via removable drives, delivered by other malware, vulnerability exploitation.

subtasks.title: Check for insider attack
subtasks.content: Checked corporate emails to determine whether it was an insider attack where employee is solicited to install ransomware.

subtasks.title: Check for stolen devices
subtasks.content: Checked whether any previously lost or stolen devices could have divulged credentials for system access.

subtasks.title: Check for human error
subtasks.content: Checked whether human error was involved.

tasks.title: Send Communications
tasks.content: Communications will need to exist for both internal and external parties. Timely and controlled external communications play a crucial role in managing the narrative.
outcomes: Approved external parties are contacted and notified of ransomware incident
Decision made whether to utilize ORNA templates for communication
Employees are notified about the incident and instructed on the next steps

subtasks.title: Work with Legal Counsel
subtasks.content: Worked with Legal Counsel and provided all available details about the incident severity, impact and the type of information involved, if known.

subtasks.title: Craft external messages
subtasks.content: Worked closely with Legal Counsel and Communications Team to craft messages to external parties to be notified.

subtasks.title: Craft internal messages
subtasks.content: Worked closely with Legal Counsel and Communications Team to craft messages for internal staff.

subtasks.title: Utilize ORNA templates
subtasks.content: Utilized ORNA communications templates where appropriate.

subtasks.title: Publish external communications
subtasks.content: Published external communications in line with the communications strategy to provide advice to affected customers or business partners.

subtasks.title: Work on communications cadence
subtasks.content: Worked with communications team and legal counsel on a cadence for updating external and internal communications.

subtasks.title: Consider regulatory requirements
subtasks.content: Considered regulatory requirements when issuing incident updates (PIPEDA, GDPR, PCI DSS, NYDFS, FFIEC, NCUA, OSFI, GLBA, SOX, HIPAA/PHIPA).

subtasks.title: Deploy internal alert page
subtasks.content: Deployed an internal alert/warning page containing information about the current ransomware attack to inform employees.

tasks.title: Invoke Data Breach Notification Protocols
tasks.content: When a cyber incident is suspected or confirmed, it is important that the Legal Counsel proceeds swiftly to understand the type of data affected.
outcomes: The type of breach and affected data are established and confirmed
Appropriate data breach notification protocols are invoked

subtasks.title: Confirm PII/PHI/financial data impact
subtasks.content: Confirmed that Personal Identifiable Information (PII), Protected Health Information (PHI), or financial information is impacted.

subtasks.title: Examine regulatory obligations
subtasks.content: Examined regulatory obligations.

subtasks.title: Notify appropriate parties
subtasks.content: Notified the appropriate regulatory agencies and affected individuals as prescribed by relevant laws, regulations and contractual obligations.

subtasks.title: Advise on client-attorney privilege
subtasks.content: Advised the CIRT members on proper communication methods to ensure continuous client-attorney privilege where possible.

phases.name: Containment
phases.description: The main objective of the Containment stage is to limit the impact of the incident promptly and as much as possible, preventing further damage to the organization's assets.

tasks.title: Disconnect Affected Assets from the Network
tasks.content: Ransomware typically spreads through the network without any user interaction. It is crucial to immediately disconnect all devices that have been infected with ransomware from the rest of the corporate network.

subtasks.title: Preserve forensic evidence
subtasks.content: Preserved forensic evidence, such as system images, before restarting, shutting down, or modifying configurations of the affected systems and assets.

subtasks.title: Disconnect infected systems
subtasks.content: Disconnected the infected systems, shared drives and devices from any network connection to reduce the risk of the infection spreading to other connected devices.

subtasks.title: Block added communications
subtasks.content: Blocked added communications associated with infected systems to contain the ransomware infection. Considerations include disabling VPN access, remote access servers, single sign on resources, and cloud-based or public-facing assets.

subtasks.title: Upload forensics evidence
subtasks.content: Attached the applicable forensics evidence (such as log files or screenshots) to this task using the Upload Artifact feature.

tasks.title: Block Current Network Communication Method
tasks.content: If the network communication method used by the malware can be determined and blocked between network segments, you should proceed to temporarily (or permanently) block this method.
outcomes: All identified malicious network artifacts are blocked

subtasks.title: Block network communication methods
subtasks.content: Temporarily blocked or disabled all relevant network communication methods that could be used by the ransomware to move laterally through the network. Examples include RDP, SMB, SIP, SSH, VNC.

tasks.title: Scan the Network for Additional Malware
tasks.content: Many ransomware campaigns utilize various malware families that threat actors use to ensure persistence on the network or to perform additional tasks.
outcomes: All additional malware is detected and eliminated

subtasks.title: Ensure antivirus up to date
subtasks.content: Ensured that antivirus signatures were up to date and that the antivirus software is installed and enabled on all assets.

subtasks.title: Confirm latest patches deployed
subtasks.content: Confirmed that the latest patches, in particular those relating to security updates, were deployed on all assets (prioritizing the targeted devices, software, operating systems, etc.)

subtasks.title: Run antivirus scan
subtasks.content: Ran an antivirus scan on the network and quarantined or removed any additional detected malware.

subtasks.title: Preserve findings
subtasks.content: Preserved all findings as evidence using the Upload Artifact feature of this Task.

tasks.title: Invoke Business Continuity Plan
tasks.content: To avoid major disruptions of key business functions, invoke the Business Continuity Plan (BCP), if such exists, in accordance with the incident severity and impact.
outcomes: If it exists the Business Continuity Plan is invoked
If no Business Continuity Plan exists a war room is used to create one
Situational business continuity procedures are established

subtasks.title: Invoke BCP communication
subtasks.content: Issued the communication and informed the appropriate stakeholders that the BCP should be invoked to minimize business disruption.

subtasks.title: Proceed with BCP procedures
subtasks.content: Proceeded in accordance with the BCP procedures.

subtasks.title: Establish war room if needed
subtasks.content: Established a war room if no BCP existed for affected systems and data. This war room would consist of technical Subject Matter Experts and business leaders to assess the most critical systems and data.

subtasks.title: Create situational procedures
subtasks.content: If no BCP existed, created situational business continuity procedures.

tasks.title: Issue External and Internal Communications Update
tasks.content: Issuing periodic updates on the progress of the incident response activities and the measures the organization is taking to contain and remediate the incident will help control the narrative.
outcomes: Internal stakeholders are informed about the ongoing containment measures
External stakeholders are informed about the ongoing containment measures

subtasks.title: Issue internal communications
subtasks.content: Issued the communications update to internal stakeholders in accordance with the communications strategy.

subtasks.title: Issue external communications
subtasks.content: Issued the communications update to external stakeholders and the media in accordance with the communications strategy.

subtasks.title: Use ORNA templates
subtasks.content: Referred to the ORNA Communications Templates section for guidance on the wording.

tasks.title: Issue Mandatory Regulatory Notifications or Updates
tasks.content: Issuing periodic updates about the progress of the incident response activities may be required based on the incident severity and the applicable laws, regulations and contractual obligations.
